mission.title: Install and Configure Open WebUI
mission.description: Open WebUI is a user-friendly AI interface that supports various LLM runners like Ollama and OpenAI-compatible APIs. It is designed for self-hosting and can operate entirely offline, making it suitable for users who require a customizable AI deployment solution. Key trade-offs include the need for local infrastructure and potential complexity in setup compared to fully managed solutions.

steps.title: Install the CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Open WebUI CLI tool, which is necessary for managing the application.

```bash

curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui

```

> This command downloads the CLI for Linux. Adjust for your OS if necessary.
commands: curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui

steps.title: Create the Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster with the specified version and node count in the desired region.

```bash

gcloud container clusters create open-webui-cluster --cluster-version=1.30 --num-nodes=3 --region=us-central1

```

> Ensure you have gcloud installed and authenticated.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command configures your local kubeconfig file to connect to the newly created cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials open-webui-cluster --region=us-central1

```

> This command fetches the credentials for your cluster.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your cluster to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see all nodes in the 'Ready' state.

steps.title: Install Open WebUI
steps.description:
This step installs Open WebUI using Helm, which simplifies the deployment process.

```bash

helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

helm install open-webui open-webui/open-webui --version 0.8.9 --namespace open-webui --create-namespace

```

> Add the Open WebUI Helm repository.
> This command installs Open WebUI in its own namespace.
commands: helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step configures autoscaling and monitoring for your Open WebUI deployment.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-webui/open-webui/main/deploy/autoscale.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-webui/open-webui/main/deploy/monitoring.yaml

```

> This command sets up autoscaling for the deployment.
> This command sets up monitoring for the deployment.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Open WebUI
uninstall.description:
This command removes Open WebUI from your cluster. Be aware that this will delete all associated resources, which may include persistent volumes and load balancers.

```bash

helm uninstall open-webui --namespace open-webui

kubectl delete namespace open-webui

```

> This command uninstalls the Open WebUI release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Open WebUI
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This process includes draining nodes if necessary, performing the upgrade, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get pvc --namespace open-webui

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade open-webui open-webui/open-webui --version 0.8.9 --namespace open-webui

kubectl get pods --namespace open-webui

```

> Check for persistent volume claims to back up.
> Replace <node-name> with the name of the node to drain.
> This command upgrades Open WebUI to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified image may not exist or the repository may be unreachable.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and ensure the repository is accessible.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The application may be crashing due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the application logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' and adjust configurations or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources allocated for the deployment.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster or adjust resource requests in the deployment configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Connection refused'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The service may not be exposed correctly or the application may not be running.

**Fix:**
Check the service configuration and ensure the application pods are running.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Ollama models, improved API integration features, and enhanced user permissions management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see output from 'kubectl get pods --namespace open-webui' showing all pods in the 'Running' state.
codeSnippets: curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui
helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before proceeding with the installation.
